cron.untrackedTooltip: This job is in pg_cron but not in the application registry. Add it to lib/cron/registry.ts or to a module manifest.
cron.noMetadata: No metadata. Register this job in lib/cron/registry.ts (core) or in the matching module manifest to add a description.
cron.expectedCommandTitle: Expected command (template from your site domain)
cron.expectedCommandHint: Generated from settings.app_domain. Replace <CRON_SECRET> with the real bearer in Supabase SQL Editor before running.
cron.urlDriftTooltip: The URL registered in pg_cron differs from what your current site domain would generate. After a domain change or rebrand, re-schedule the job from the SQL Editor using the expected command above.
cron.guideIntro: The actual cron schedule is owned by Supabase pg_cron, not by the app. To change frequency or disable a job, run a SQL command in the Supabase SQL Editor.
cron.guideAlterIntro: Example: switch from every 5 minutes to every minute.
cron.guideUnscheduleIntro: Drop the job from pg_cron entirely.
cron.guideTip1: Cron schedules use standard crontab syntax: minute, hour, day of month, month, day of week.
cron.guideTip2: Run every minute: "* * * * *". Every 5 min: "*/5 * * * *". Top of every hour: "0 * * * *".
cron.guideTip3: After alter_job, the table above will reflect the new schedule on the next page refresh.

gdpr-export-worker.label: GDPR Export Worker
gdpr-export-worker.description: Processes pending GDPR data-export jobs (max 5 per run) and deletes export files whose expires_at is past.
gdpr-export-worker.purpose: Required by GDPR. Users can request a copy of their data from /settings/privacy; this cron generates the ZIP, emails the link to the user, and later cleans up expired files.

sessions-cleanup.label: Sessions Cleanup
sessions-cleanup.description: Deletes server-side session rows that are expired or have been revoked more than the grace window ago.
sessions-cleanup.purpose: Keeps the sessions table bounded and removes stale records of logged-out / expired devices. Backs the active-sessions UI in /settings/security and the auto-logout-elsewhere flow.

soft-deleted-purge.label: Soft-deleted Account Purge
soft-deleted-purge.description: Hard-deletes user rows that have been soft-deleted (deleted_at IS NOT NULL) for more than 30 days.
soft-deleted-purge.purpose: Implements the GDPR 30-day grace window after an account-deletion request. Within 30 days the user can still recover the account; after that the row is purged and FK ON DELETE SET NULL preserves audit trails.

notifications-dispatch.label: Admin Notifications Dispatch
notifications-dispatch.description: Runs all notification generators (cron failures, secret rotation, …) and reconciles admin_notifications: inserts new alerts, refreshes severity, auto-resolves conditions that have cleared.
notifications-dispatch.purpose: Ensures admin alerts (e.g. a cron going into failure) appear within minutes instead of waiting for an admin to navigate the panel. The layout-render trigger still acts as a fallback if pg_cron is not running.

notifications-email-dispatch.label: Admin Notifications Email Dispatch
notifications-email-dispatch.description: Generic email dispatcher for admin_notifications. Iterates the registered alert sources (sessions, cron, …), honours the per-source schedule (instant / hourly / daily) and severity threshold, groups pending notifications by type, renders the type-specific template and sends a digest email. Marks email_sent_at on the delivered rows so they are not re-sent.
notifications-email-dispatch.purpose: Single source of truth for outbound admin emails: any new notification source just registers a renderer instead of wiring its own pipeline. Recipients, schedules and thresholds are tunable per-source from /admin/settings/notifications.

sessions-suspicious-detection.label: Suspicious Sessions Detection
sessions-suspicious-detection.description: Runs the configured Tier-1 heuristics (multiple IPs, concurrent devices, bot UA, failed→success login, sensitive action on new IP, …) over recent sessions / login_attempts / activity_logs and persists candidates into session_alerts. Sends an email digest to admins when the schedule allows.
sessions-suspicious-detection.purpose: Surfaces compromised or hijacked sessions early. Detect-only by default — alerts appear in /admin/access/sessions and trigger an admin notification + email digest, but do not auto-revoke sessions. Tunable from /admin/settings/notifications.

policy-change-notifications.label: Policy Change Notifications
policy-change-notifications.description: Selects up to 50 distinct users with pending rows in policy_change_notifications and emails each one a single digest of all their out-of-date policies (terms, privacy, marketing). Marks rows sent/failed with retry logic (max 3 attempts).
policy-change-notifications.purpose: Required by GDPR re-consent flow. When an admin bumps a system policy version, all affected users get a row in policy_change_notifications; this cron delivers the email so they know to re-accept on the next login. Frequency tunable via gdpr.policy.notifications_cron_minutes (default 60).

consent-records-cleanup.label: Consent Records Retention Cleanup
consent-records-cleanup.description: Deletes rows from consent_records older than gdpr.consent_log.retention_after_deletion_days (default 5 years), in batches of 5000 with a 20-batch cap per run (~100k rows max per execution). Backlog drains across subsequent runs.
consent-records-cleanup.purpose: Bounds the append-only consent ledger so it doesn't grow indefinitely. The retention window matches the typical GDPR Art. 7(1) demonstrability period; older rows have no probative value and can be dropped. Cron suggested daily at 03:00 UTC; set retention to 0 to disable.

ip-rules-maintenance.label: IP Rules Maintenance
ip-rules-maintenance.description: Two jobs in one: deletes expired ip_rules rows older than 30 days, and flushes per-rule hit counters from Redis (GETDEL on ip-rule:hits:<id>) into the hit_count column.
ip-rules-maintenance.purpose: Keeps the manual IP rules table tidy (auto-purge of long-stale expired entries) and persists analytics counters that are written fire-and-forget at request time. Without this cron, the dashboard would show zero hits even for rules that matched many times. Suggested every 15 minutes.

modules-prices-sync.label: Prices Sync
modules-prices-sync.description: Fetches the latest prices for the active coin universe from CoinGecko / fallback sources and updates the live KV cache.
modules-prices-sync.purpose: Keeps live prices fresh on the frontend (markets table, watchlists, charts).

modules-prices-snapshot.label: Prices Snapshot
modules-prices-snapshot.description: Persists a periodic snapshot of prices into the time-series table for historical charts.
modules-prices-snapshot.purpose: Powers historical charts and delta computations (24h, 7d).

modules-prices-cleanup.label: Prices Cleanup
modules-prices-cleanup.description: Deletes price snapshots older than the configured retention window from the time-series table.
modules-prices-cleanup.purpose: Keeps DB size bounded and respects the retention policy in module settings.

welcome.label: Welcome email
welcome.defaultBody:
Hi '{{userName}}',

Welcome to '{{appName}}'! Your account has been created successfully.

You can sign in to the platform from: '{{appUrl}}'

signup.label: Signup verification
signup.defaultBody:
Hi '{{userName}}',

Use the code below to verify your account.
The code is valid for 15 minutes.

Code: '{{otpCode}}'

reset.label: Password Reset

Hi '{{userName}}',

You requested to reset your account password.
Click the link below to proceed. The link is valid for 30 minutes.

'{{resetLink}}'

deleted.label: User deleted
deleted.defaultBody:
Hi '{{userName}}',

Your '{{appName}}' account was permanently deleted on '{{deletedDate}}' by an administrator.

Your personal data has been removed from active systems.

waitinglist.label: Waiting list (landing coming-soon)
waitinglist.defaultBody:
Hi,

Thanks for joining the '{{appName}}' waiting list.

You'll be among the first to know when we open the doors — we'll write as soon as we're ready.

See you soon.

emailchange.label: Email change verification
emailchange.defaultSubject: '{{otpCode}}' is your code to confirm the new '{{appName}}' email
emailchange.defaultBody:
Hi '{{userName}}',

You requested to change your '{{appName}}' account email to this address. Enter the code below to confirm the change. If this wasn't you, ignore this email — the change won't be applied.

Code: '{{otpCode}}'

device.label: Device verification (login from new device)
device.defaultSubject: '{{otpCode}}' is your sign-in code from a new device
device.defaultBody:
Hi '{{userName}}',

We detected a sign-in to your '{{appName}}' account from an unrecognised device. Enter the code below to confirm it's you.

Code: '{{otpCode}}'

staffinvite.label: Staff invitation
staffinvite.defaultBody:
Hi,

'{{inviterName}}' invited you to join the '{{appName}}' staff team with the role of '{{roleLabel}}'.

Click the button below to accept or decline the invitation. The link is valid for 48 hours.

gdprexport.label: GDPR export ready
gdprexport.defaultBody:
We've prepared the archive of your personal data on '{{appName}}', as you requested.
You can download it from the button below. The link is valid for 24 hours; if it expires, you can regenerate it from the privacy settings of your account.

accountdeletion.label: Account deletion requested
accountdeletion.defaultBody:
We've received your account deletion request on '{{appName}}'. Your personal data will be permanently deleted on '{{purgeDate}}'.
Until then you can cancel the request by contacting support. After the purge it will no longer be possible to recover the data.

accountdeletionotp.label: Account deletion OTP (OAuth-only)
accountdeletionotp.defaultSubject: '{{otpCode}}' is your code to delete your '{{appName}}' account
accountdeletionotp.defaultBody: You requested to delete your '{{appName}}' account. Enter the code below to confirm. If this wasn't you, ignore this email — the code expires in 15 minutes.

mfaenabled.label: MFA enabled (two-factor authentication)
mfaenabled.defaultBody:
Hi '{{userName}}',

You just enabled two-factor authentication on your '{{appName}}' account. From now on at sign-in we'll ask for a code generated by your authenticator app in addition to your password.

Keep your recovery codes in a safe place: they're the only way to sign in if you lose your phone.

If this wasn't you, sign in to your account, disable two-factor authentication and contact support immediately.

mfadisabled.label: MFA disabled (two-factor authentication)
mfadisabled.defaultBody:
Hi '{{userName}}',

You just disabled two-factor authentication on your '{{appName}}' account. At the next sign-in we'll only use email and password — your account's protection level has decreased.

All the recovery codes you had have been invalidated. You can re-enable two-factor authentication at any time from your security settings.

If this wasn't you, change your password immediately and contact support.

mfaadminreset.label: MFA reset by admin (support reset user TOTP)
mfaadminreset.defaultBody:
Hi '{{userName}}',

A '{{appName}}' administrator reset two-factor authentication on your account. All previous recovery codes have been invalidated and at the next sign-in we'll only use email and password.

Support reason: '{{reason}}'

To restore protection, sign in to your account and re-enable two-factor authentication from your security settings. If you didn't request this operation, contact support immediately and change your password.

modstrike.label: Moderation · strike received
modstrike.defaultBody:
Hi '{{userName}}',

One of our moderators reviewed a report on one of your '{{sourceType}}' and accepted it. The content has been removed and you received a moderation strike.

Active strikes on your account: '{{strikeNumber}}'/3.
Reason: '{{reason}}'.

When you reach 3 active strikes the account is automatically suspended. Please review our community guidelines to avoid further violations.
modstrike.defaultFooter: © '{{appName}}' · Automated email, please do not reply

modbanned.label: Moderation · account suspended (3 strikes)
modbanned.defaultBody:
Hi '{{userName}}',

Following three accumulated moderation strikes, your account on '{{appName}}' has been suspended and you can no longer access the platform.

The last '{{sourceType}}' that triggered the suspension has been removed. Final reason: '{{reason}}'.

If you believe this decision is unfair you can contact support by replying to this email or via the contact form on the site.
modbanned.defaultFooter: © '{{appName}}' · Automated email, please do not reply

modstrikerevoked.label: Moderation · strike revoked
modstrikerevoked.defaultSubject: A strike was revoked on your '{{appName}}' account
modstrikerevoked.defaultBody:
Hi '{{userName}}',

One of our moderators revoked one of your strikes after reviewing the case.

Remaining active strikes: '{{activeCountAfter}}'/3.

If your account was suspended and the revocation brought the counter back below 3, access has been automatically reinstated.
modstrikerevoked.defaultFooter: © '{{appName}}' · Automated email, please do not reply

cross_user_campaign.label: Cross-user campaign

validation.description: Zod schema validates username (3–50 chars), email format, password (min 8, uppercase, number, special char), and the three consent checkboxes (terms, privacy, marketing).

registrationsEnabled.description: Master switch checked from app_settings. If disabled, the sign-up form errors out immediately.

turnstile.label: Cloudflare Turnstile verify
turnstile.description: Anti-bot token validated server-side. Silently disabled when keys are not configured.

rateLimit.label: Sign-up rate limit
rateLimit.description: Per-IP throttle: max bf_signup_max attempts in bf_window_minutes. Blocks both abuse and brute-force enumeration.

blacklists.label: Blacklist gates (parallel)
blacklists.description: Three independent denylists run in parallel: IP (Redis-cached), disposable email domains (Postgres), reserved usernames (in-memory).

availability.label: Availability checks (parallel)
availability.description: Email + username uniqueness checked via Bloom filter (fast no-network) then DB (authoritative). Cuts most duplicates before touching DB.

consents.label: Read consent versions
consents.description: Latest terms / privacy / marketing versions resolved from CMS pages — saved alongside the user record so audits can prove what they accepted.

hashPassword.label: Hash password
hashPassword.description: bcrypt hash computed server-side. The plaintext never touches the database.

insertUser.label: Insert users + profiles
insertUser.description: Two related rows in a near-atomic sequence. UNIQUE constraints catch any race condition (rare but real).

bloomUpdate.label: Bloom filter update
bloomUpdate.description: Email + username added to the per-process bloom filter so the next sign-up sees them instantly without an extra DB round-trip.

consentLedger.label: GDPR consent ledger
consentLedger.description: Append-only consent record (Art. 7). IP + UA captured per the strategy configured in /admin/compliance/gdpr.

sendEmail.label: Send verification email
sendEmail.description: 6-digit code created and sent via Resend. The user lands on the verify-email page after submit.

guide.intro: This dashboard cross-references the local package.json + pnpm-lock.yaml with the npm registry, GitHub Releases / changelogs, and any Dependabot PRs already open against this repo. The goal is not just to show what's outdated but to quantify the risk of each upgrade so you can decide which ones to merge today and which to schedule.
guide.riskCurrent: Current — installed version equals npm latest. Nothing to do.
guide.riskLow: Low — patch or minor bump, no breaking changes flagged, Dependabot CI green (when a PR exists). Usually safe to merge.
guide.riskMedium: Medium — minor with breaking flags, or any bump where the Dependabot CI is failing. Read the changelog before merging.
guide.riskHigh: High — major bump. Likely requires code changes; schedule it.
guide.riskVulnerable: Vulnerable — Dependabot opened a security PR for this package. Merge as soon as CI is green, regardless of the bump type.
guide.dependabotBody: Dependabot opens PRs every Monday morning (config in <c>.github/dependabot.yml</c>). When a PR exists for a package, this dashboard surfaces its number, its CI status, and a direct link. Use the per-package <c>PR #N</c> link to jump in and merge.
guide.breakingBody: We fetch the package's CHANGELOG from GitHub (<c>main</c> or <c>master</c>) and grep the slice between the installed and the new version for keywords like <c>BREAKING CHANGE</c>, <c>removed</c>, <c>deprecated</c>. It's a heuristic — always click through to the changelog before merging a major bump. The detection is skipped for patches (rarely contain breaking changes by semver convention).
guide.securityBody: Security PRs from GitHub bypass the weekly Dependabot schedule and appear here labelled <c>vulnerable</c>. The dashboard does not actively poll the GitHub Advisory Database — we rely on Dependabot to surface CVEs as PRs.

guide.intro: This page surfaces every active and historical login session, plus the suspicious-session alerts produced by the Tier-1 detection pipeline. Below is the operator's guide: how it performs, what to monitor, and how to tune it if anything ever drifts.
guide.perfPublic: Public app: zero. No detector touches user-facing requests — sign-in, page loads and session validation are unchanged.
guide.perfAdmin: Admin pages: +1 indexed COUNT(*) GROUP BY severity when this page renders. Trivial cost (idx_session_alerts_unack).
guide.perfCron: Cron sessions-suspicious-detection: runs every 15 minutes out-of-band. ~14 SQL queries over short time windows (24h max) plus pipelined Redis ops. Never blocks user requests.
guide.monitorIntro: One metric matters: the <c>durationMs</c> returned by the cron route. Inspect it in <c>cron.job_run_details</c> on Supabase, in Vercel function logs, or in <c>/admin/settings/cron</c>.
guide.monitorYellow: 10–30 s — keep an eye, especially as sessions volume grows.
guide.detectorsHeading: Detectors that grow heavier at scale
guide.detectorsIntro: Ten of the thirteen heuristics scan only the last 24h or current active sessions — they stay cheap. These three may need attention past a few thousand active sessions:
guide.detectorLongIdle: long_idle_resurrect — keeps a Redis snapshot per active session. Already pipelined: one MGET + one batched SET-EX per tick, so the cost is ~2 round-trips per 500 sessions instead of 2× per session. If it ever creeps up, tighten the SQL window further.
guide.detectorOffBaseline: off_baseline_hours — recomputes a per-user hour percentile over 30 days each tick. Mitigation: cache the baseline in Redis with a 24h TTL (a future optimization).
guide.detectorNewSubnet: new_subnet — aggregates 90 days of session history per recently active user. Mitigation: lower lookbackDays in settings (default 90), or precompute a per-user 'known subnets' set.
guide.mitDisable: Disable a single rule — the offending heuristic stops running, the rest keeps working.
guide.mitTune: Tune thresholds per rule — raise count, lower windowHours, etc. Higher thresholds = fewer alerts.
guide.mitDryRun: Dry-run mode — alerts are logged for audit but no email or panel notification is sent. Useful when tuning thresholds without spamming admins.
guide.mitEmailOff: Email schedule = off — silence the digest while still seeing alerts in the Alerts tab and the bell.
guide.mitSeverity: Severity threshold — bump to warning or critical to suppress info-level noise.
guide.troubleAlertsGrowing: <c>session_alerts</c> growing fast → likely a noisy rule with thresholds too low.
guide.troubleSqlMissing: Many <c>cron.job_run_details</c> failures with <c>relation 'session_alerts' does not exist</c> → SQL migration not applied.
guide.troubleRedisErr: Redis errors in Vercel logs around <c>[detect/long_idle_resurrect]</c> → Upstash quota or connectivity issue. Detector fails closed (skips the tick).
guide.flowHeading: How alerts flow through the system
guide.flowDetect: Cron runs the 13 detectors → each returns candidate alerts with a deterministic <c>dedup_key</c>.
guide.flowInsert: Insert into <c>session_alerts</c> with <c>ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING</c> — same incident never duplicates.
guide.flowEmail: If above the severity threshold and not in dry-run, an email digest is queued (instant / hourly / daily).
guide.flowDispatcher: The notifications dispatcher picks up unacknowledged alerts and shows them in the bell, grouped by severity.
guide.flowReview: An admin reviews the Alerts tab, acknowledges or revokes the session — the panel notification auto-resolves when the bucket empties.
guide.flowCallout: Detect-only by design: alerts never auto-revoke a session. Either an admin acts manually, or the existing session lifecycle (idle timeout, expiry, password change) takes over.

info.triggerTitle: How does the permission system work?
info.triggerSubtitle: Quick guide, configuration, and code usage examples
info.howItWorksIntro: The RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) system is based on three overlapping layers:
info.layerPermissionsDesc: Atomic app actions. Use the resource:action pattern, e.g., posts:publish, users:ban.
info.layerRolesDesc: Sets of permissions. Assign a set of permissions to each role in Role Management.
info.layerOverridesDesc: Grant or revoke a specific permission for a single user, including expiration dates.
info.setupStep1: Go to the Permission Catalog (next tab) and create the permissions you need. Use names in the format resource:action, for example posts:publish, users:ban, admin:access.
info.setupStep2: Go to the Role Matrix and use the toggles to assign permissions to roles. Each cell is a toggle: green = permission granted.
info.setupStep3: For individual exceptions, open a user's details in Users → Access tab → Overrides section. You can grant or revoke a permission with an optional expiration date.
info.setupStep4: In your app's code, use the <c>can()</c> function to verify if the current user has a specific permission (see example below).
info.codeFootnote: The <c>can(userId, key)</c> function automatically resolves roles + individual overrides (grant/revoke) and respects expiration dates.

guide.intro: Pages are split into two tabs. Pages hosts the regular CMS content the admin creates from scratch — full content editor, templates, custom fields. System hosts a fixed set of pages that the platform needs for its own routing (auth, error pages, legal docs): they can't be deleted and have a stricter editing surface.
guide.flavorsEditorial: Content-editable (privacy, terms, cookie, marketing) — served from the CMS catch-all router. Body, title and SEO meta are editable like a normal page.
guide.flavorsMetaOnly: Meta-only (everything else: sign-in, verify-email, 404, admin, home, …) — the actual route is served by a hardcoded Next.js page handler. The CMS record is just an admin container so you can edit the title (used in the page list) and the SEO meta. Content body is hidden.
guide.editTitle: Title — always editable. It's both the public page title and the admin label in the System tab list.
guide.editSlug: Slug — always locked on system pages, regardless of flavor. The slug is the canonical English path and is uniform across every locale (the proxy redirects /en/sign-in or /it/sign-in to /sign-in). To rename a system slug you have to do it via SQL in Supabase and update the matching page handler / proxy logic.
guide.editContent: Content body — full Tiptap editor on user pages and on content-editable system pages. Hidden on meta-only system pages.
guide.editVisibility: Visibility (Public / Private) — in the Publishing tab. Private pages redirect unauthenticated visitors to /sign-in. Default is Public. (Hidden for meta-only system pages — their auth gate is enforced by the route handler itself.)
guide.editSeo: SEO meta — in the SEO tab on every page. Title, description, Open Graph, robots, JSON-LD. Stored in the seo_pages table keyed on /'{slug}'.
guide.lockedHeading: Why every system slug is locked
guide.lockedBody: Some system routes are served by a hardcoded Next.js handler (<c>/sign-in</c> → <c>app/(login)/sign-in/page.tsx</c>, <c>/404</c> → <c>app/not-found.tsx</c>). Renaming the admin record wouldn't move the real route — handler and SEO record would drift. Editorial system pages (<c>privacy</c>, <c>terms</c>, …) could technically rename safely through the CMS catch-all, but we lock them too: it keeps the policy uniform and lets us show the same slug across every locale tab without locale-specific overlays.
guide.placeholdersIntro: Both the page content and the SEO meta accept a small set of tokens, resolved server-side at render time:
guide.placeholdersYear: <c>'{{currentYear}}'</c> — current year, useful in footer copyrights.
guide.placeholdersExample: Example: writing <c>Login | '{{appName}}'</c> as the SEO title renders <c>Login | AppName</c>.
guide.dataPagesTable: <c>pages</c> table — title, content, slug, visibility, isSystem flag, systemKey. Source of truth for both the CMS router and <c>proxy.ts</c> visibility check.
guide.dataSeoTable: <c>seo_pages</c> table — meta tags, keyed by pathname. Written by the SEO tab inside this page editor.
guide.dataWhitelist: <c>SYSTEM_PAGE_KEYS_EDITABLE_SLUG</c> in <c>lib/db/schema.ts</c> — kept as an empty array by policy: every system slug is locked from the UI. Re-populating this list would re-enable per-page slug editing, but the canonical-English-slug rule would no longer hold.
guide.mistakesSeoConflict: Don't expect changes to <c>/admin/seo/…</c> for a page slug to outrank what you set in this editor's SEO tab — both write to the same row in <c>seo_pages</c>, last write wins.
guide.schemaBody: The full enum of system identifiers is <c>SYSTEM_PAGE_KEYS</c> in <c>lib/db/schema.ts</c>. Adding a new system page means adding the key to that array, adding a row to <c>pages</c> via a migration with <c>is_system = true</c>, and (for meta-only) deciding whether the slug should be in the editable whitelist.

media.pageSubtitle: Upload and manage images, videos and documents reusable across pages and templates
media.guideIntro: We never serve the raw original to visitors. Each upload stays untouched in storage; on render we generate resized variants on the fly through Vercel's image optimizer (or Supabase Image Transformations if enabled), then cache them on the CDN edge.
media.guideBulletOriginal: The original file you upload is preserved in the bucket and used only as the source for transformation. Lightbox click-to-zoom loads a 1920px variant, never the raw file.
media.guideBulletVariants: Each render point uses a preset (width list + quality + sizes hint). The browser picks the smallest variant matching its viewport via srcset.
media.guideBulletWhereCmsBody: Inline images in article body: 360 / 640 / 1024 / 1440px, quality 75.
media.guideBulletWhereAdmin: Admin grid + picker thumbs: 400px (single variant, low traffic).
media.guideTuning: To change widths, quality or sizes for a preset edit lib/storage/image-widths.ts. After deploying, the first request to a new preset triggers transcoding — subsequent requests hit the CDN cache.

blockedDomains.pageSubtitle: Manage disposable email domains blocked from registration.

guide.intro: This list rejects sign-up attempts whose email address belongs to a domain you've added here. Useful to fight registrations from disposable / throwaway providers (e.g. <c>mailinator.com</c>, <c>10minutemail.com</c>) without writing custom validation.
guide.howBody: When a user submits the sign-up form, the email is split on @ and the domain is lower-cased and looked up exactly in the list. Subdomains do not match a parent entry — adding example.com doesn't block foo.example.com; add the subdomains explicitly if you need to.
guide.whenBody: Only at sign-up time, before account creation. Existing users are not affected — if you block a domain after they registered, they keep their account but can no longer create a new one with that domain. Login is not gated by this list.
guide.examplesBody: Domains that abuse your sign-up flow: temp-mail providers, disposable forwarding services, single-use catch-alls. Don't add common providers (gmail, outlook, ...) — you'd block legitimate users. The bulk-import box accepts one domain per line and dedupes silently.
guide.dependenciesBody: None beyond Postgres. The lookup is a single indexed query on disposable_domains, no Redis, no in-memory cache, no external API. Adding/removing entries is immediately effective on the next sign-up attempt.

blockedDomains.bulkHelp: One domain per line. Duplicates are ignored.

blockedUsernames.pageSubtitle: Manage reserved usernames that cannot be used during registration.

guide.intro: This list rejects sign-up attempts whose chosen username matches an entry. Lookups are case-insensitive. Two kinds of entries are supported: exact matches for reserved handles, and wildcard patterns for variants.
guide.howBody: On sign-up the username is lower-cased and compared against every entry. An exact entry matches only the literal string; a pattern entry uses <c>*</c> as a placeholder for any characters and supports three positions (see below).
guide.patternsBody: *word* = contains (matches if word appears anywhere). word* = starts with. *word = ends with. Without any asterisk the entry is treated as exact. Use the Pattern toggle in the add form to enable wildcards — the UI will wrap your input in *…* automatically.
guide.whenBody: Only at sign-up time, before account creation. Existing users keep their handles even if you block them retroactively. The list is also queried by the username availability check in the sign-up form, so users see the rejection inline instead of on submit.
guide.dependenciesBody: Postgres + an in-process cache. The full list is loaded once into memory and refreshed every 5 minutes; admin add/remove invalidates the cache so changes take effect on the very next request. No Redis, no external API. The cache is per-process — in serverless environments each warm instance has its own copy.

blockedUsernames.bulkHelp: One username per line. Use <c>*word*</c> for patterns. Duplicates are ignored.

ipRules.pageSubtitle: Manage allow/deny rules for IP addresses and ranges

guide.intro: IP rules let you allow or deny specific addresses or CIDR ranges. They apply at two distinct layers, chosen per rule via the Scope field.
guide.authBody: Rules with scope Auth apply to signup, login, and email/username availability checks. An allow rule bypasses bruteforce limits and rate-limit thresholds; a deny rule rejects the request immediately, without ever touching Redis or the database.
guide.adminBody: Rules with scope Admin only matter when Admin Lockdown is on (top toggle). When the lockdown is enabled, the admin panel returns 404 for any IP not covered by an admin allow rule. Two safety checks prevent self-lockout: you can't enable the lockdown if your current IP isn't already allowed, and you can't remove the rule that's currently letting you in.
guide.precedenceBody: If multiple rules match the same IP, allow wins. Useful pattern: deny a wide CIDR (e.g. an abusive provider) and allow a narrow one inside it (your VPN exit node).
guide.cidrBody: Single addresses and CIDR ranges are supported for both IPv4 and IPv6. Use <c>1.2.3.4</c> for one host, <c>192.168.0.0/24</c> for an office subnet, <c>2a01::/32</c> for a v6 prefix. Validation runs server-side.
guide.perfBody: Rules are loaded into memory once every 30 seconds via a tagged cache, then matched in microseconds with a BigInt CIDR comparator — the request hot path never queries the database. Hit counters are written fire-and-forget on Redis and flushed in batch by a cron, so analytics never slow anything down. With Admin Lockdown off (the default), this whole subsystem costs the proxy a single cached settings lookup.

guide.intro: The cookie banner respects four GDPR/ePrivacy categories: <c>cookie_necessary</c>, <c>cookie_preferences</c>, <c>cookie_analytics</c>, <c>cookie_marketing</c>. Every tracker the platform uses must be declared here so the admin matrix, the public banner copy, and the consent ledger stay aligned without scattered hardcoded checks.
guide.addServiceHeading: How to add a new service
guide.addStep1: Click Add service on the right category and fill in: a unique id (lowercase + underscore), provider name and provider policy URL (for third-party trackers), and a localized name + description for each enabled language.
guide.addStep2: Pick the right category. Marketing pixels and ad retargeting go in <c>cookie_marketing</c>. Anonymous page-view counters go in <c>cookie_analytics</c>. UI persistence (theme, locale, saved filters) goes in <c>cookie_preferences</c>. Session, CSRF and consent storage go in <c>cookie_necessary</c>.
guide.addStep3Mid: After saving, gate the actual script load on the user's consent. Pattern (in app/layout.tsx or wherever the script is mounted):
guide.addStep3Footer: Without this gate the registry is just documentation: the cookie would be set regardless of opt-in and the platform would be non-compliant.
guide.firstPartyDesc: First-party — cookies we set ourselves, served from our domain. Examples: session cookie, CSRF token, consent state. Don't need a provider or policy URL.
guide.thirdPartyDesc: Third-party — cookies set by an external SDK loaded on the page. Always fill in provider (legal entity name, not just the product) and the provider policy URL — both end up in the public banner under the category.
guide.matrixHeading: How to read the matrix below
guide.matrixAlwaysOn: Always on — the category is technical (Art. 5(3) ePrivacy). It runs even with the banner disabled. Logged in the ledger as granted for audit completeness.
guide.matrixBlocked: Blocked (banner OFF) — the master switch is off, so this non-essential category can never opt in. Useful while you're still drafting the cookie policy.
guide.matrixUserOptIn: User opt-in — the master switch is on; whether the services run depends on each visitor's choice on the banner.
guide.mistakesCallout: Adding a service to the registry but forgetting to gate its script load is the most frequent regression. The registry is documentation — it does not automatically prevent the cookie from being set. Always pair the registry change with a consent check at the mount site.
guide.mistakeMisuse: Don't put a tracker in cookie_necessary just because "the site needs it to work the way we want". Necessary means the user requested it (session, CSRF). Analytics never qualifies — even when business-critical.
guide.mistakeRemoval: When you delete a service from this view, remove the script-load site at the same commit too. A live <c>'<script src>'</c> with no matching registry entry leaves visitors with cookies they never agreed to.
guide.schemaBody: The four <c>consent_type</c> values come from <c>CONSENT_TYPES</c> in <c>lib/db/schema.ts</c> and are persisted in <c>consent_records</c> with <c>source = "cookie_banner"</c> and <c>metadata.variant ∈ {literalOpen}accept_all, reject_all, custom{literalClose}</c>. Adding a fifth category is a schema change (new row in <c>cookie_categories</c> + new value in the enum + i18n key + service routing) — not just a UI change.

settings.consentLogIntro: Append-only audit trail of every consent event (granted / revoked). Required for GDPR Art. 7(1) — "the controller shall be able to demonstrate that the data subject has consented".
settings.backupIntro: Declare the backup tier protecting consent records. The dashboard shows a warning when consent logging is enabled but no backup tier is declared.
settings.lifecycleIntro: Timing for account deletion grace period and data export cooldown.
settings.policyIntro: Behavior when policies (Terms / Privacy) are bumped to a new version. Cookie banner has its own dedicated section.
settings.enabledHint: Master switch. When ON, every consent action is written to a dedicated append-only table. Without this you cannot demonstrate consent under Art. 7(1).
settings.captureIpTitle: Capture client IP at consent time
settings.captureIpHint: Stores the IP from x-forwarded-for at consent time — without it the ledger row lacks a key piece of evidence the supervisory authority expects to see.
settings.ipStrategyHint: How the IP is stored. Required by Art. 5(1)(c) data minimisation: full raw IP is acceptable only with a documented justification, otherwise prefer mask or hash.
settings.uaHint: Useful as corroborating evidence; truncated to 512 chars by the consent writer. Not strictly required.
settings.hashPolicyHint: Stores SHA-256 of the exact policy text the user saw. Strongly advised: protects against later tampering of pages.content / page_versions.content.
settings.retentionHint: Maximum age (days) for any row in consent_records. Older rows are deleted by the daily consent-records-cleanup cron. Set 0 to disable. Default 1825 = 5 years (matches the typical GDPR Art. 7(1) demonstrability window).
settings.backupTierHint: Declares the operational backup tier. Art. 32 expects you to document the technical/organisational measures protecting the consent ledger.
settings.backupNotesPlaceholder: e.g. Supabase Pro PITR 7d + nightly pg_dump to s3://gencry-backup/
settings.backupNotesHint: Free-text description of the backup setup. Visible to auditors in the export ledger CSV header.
settings.backupNoneWarningBody: You have no backup tier selected. This puts you in a non-compliance posture: Art. 32 requires technical measures to protect personal data, including the ability to restore availability after an incident. Pick `Supabase PITR` if your project is on a Pro plan or higher, otherwise document your external backup setup.
settings.backupPitrPaneIntro: Supabase ships PITR on Pro and higher (7-day window on Pro, configurable up to 28 days on Team/Enterprise). Click `Verify` to query the live plan via the Management API.
settings.backupPitrServiceUnconfiguredBody: Add the Supabase Personal Access Token and project ref under Services → Supabase to enable live PITR verification.
settings.backupPitrNeverChecked: Never verified — click `Verify PITR now` to populate.
settings.backupExternalPaneIntro: Document the third-party backup pipeline: provider, cadence, retention, and the last recovery test. Auditors expect at least an annual restore test on file.
settings.backupExternalProviderPlaceholder: AWS S3 / Backblaze B2 / Restic / pg_dump
settings.backupExternalRetentionHint: How long you keep backups before pruning. 0 = no retention (only the latest snapshot is kept).
settings.backupExternalLastVerifiedHint: Date you last restored a backup to verify integrity. GDPR auditors expect at least an annual test.
settings.backupExternalRecoveryNotesPlaceholder: RPO/RTO targets, test outcomes, schedule, runbook link…
settings.pitrErrorCredentialsMissing: Supabase credentials missing. Configure them under Services → Supabase first.
settings.pitrErrorInvalidToken: Supabase token is not valid or has expired. Update it under Services → Supabase.
settings.pitrErrorForbidden: Supabase token does not have access to this project.
settings.pitrErrorProjectNotFound: Supabase project not found. Check the project ref.
settings.pitrVerifiedUnsupported: Verified · plan {tier} does NOT support PITR. Upgrade or pick another tier.
settings.deletionGraceHint: Days between soft-delete (user requests deletion) and physical purge. Art. 17 requires you to honour erasure requests; the specific number of days is a policy choice. Persisted now; the deletion code reads a 30-day default until wired in a follow-up PR.
settings.exportRateHint: Anti-abuse cooldown between two export requests for the same user. Persisted now; export code reads a 7-day default until wired in a follow-up PR.
settings.forceReconsentHint: When ON, bumping a system page (terms / privacy / marketing) enqueues an email notification and a sign-in banner asking the user to re-accept the new version. EDPB guidance recommends re-consent for material policy changes.
settings.reconsentGraceHint: How long the soft banner shows before turning into a blocking modal. Counts from the moment the bump is enqueued, not from the user's first login.
settings.cronMinutesHint: How often the policy-change-notifications cron worker dispatches pending email batches. Set the matching schedule in pg_cron — this value is informational unless your scheduler reads from settings.

requirementBadge.requiredTooltip: Mandatory for GDPR compliance — turning this off (or changing it) puts you in a breach posture. See the legend (i icon next to the page title) for the article reference.
requirementBadge.recommendedTooltip: Strong best practice (EDPB / Garante guidance). Not strictly required but highly advised; deviating means you should be able to explain why.
requirementBadge.optionalTooltip: Operational preference — does not affect GDPR compliance either way.
requirementBadge.unusedTooltip: Persisted for backward compatibility but no consumer reads it in the current code path. Safe to ignore.

legendGuide.intro: Each setting below is tagged with one of four markers so it's clear at a glance what the law actually demands and what is just an operational preference. The mapping is conservative — if a setting could be argued either way, we lean toward the stricter label.
legendGuide.requiredDesc: Mandatory for GDPR compliance. Disabling or misconfiguring puts you in a breach posture — you would not be able to meet a request from the data subject or the supervisory authority.
legendGuide.recommendedDesc: Strong best practice from EDPB / Garante guidance. Not strict law text, but if you deviate you should be able to justify why during an audit.
legendGuide.optionalDesc: Operational preference. Switching it does not affect GDPR compliance — it only changes the user experience or the operational bookkeeping.
legendGuide.unusedDesc: Setting is persisted for backward compatibility but no code path reads it any more. Will be removed in a future cleanup.
legendGuide.art7: Art. 7(1) — "the controller shall be able to demonstrate that the data subject has consented". This is why the consent ledger itself (and the metadata that make a record probative — IP, IP strategy) are Required.
legendGuide.art17: Art. 17 — Right to erasure. A working deletion grace period and the ability to wipe consent records on request are Required; the specific number of days is operational.
legendGuide.eprivacy: ePrivacy Directive (2002/58/EC) + GDPR — non-technical cookies require prior, opt-in consent collected through a banner. That makes the cookie banner master switch Required as soon as you ship to EU traffic.
legendGuide.caveatBody: These markers are a pragmatic shortcut, not legal advice. For a GDPR audit, ground-truth is the regulation text and your DPO / legal counsel — the labels here help you focus their attention.

setupGuide.intro: This widget pulls unresolved issues from the Sentry API. It needs three project environment variables on your hosting platform (the first two are already used by the build plugin):

env.name: SENTRY_API_AUTH_TOKEN
env.hint: User Auth Token. Required scopes: Project: Read + Issue & Event: Read. Optional: Issue & Event: Write to enable the Resolve button. Falls back to SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN if missing.
